result:
I. The Curtain Has No Window

The first time I understood that I was alive, I was staring at a brown paper grocery bag that someone had flattened and slipped between the iron rails of a radiator. The bag had dried, puckered, and tightened into the shape of a small abstract sculpture, something halfway between a lung and a desert rose. I was six, maybe seven. The radiator hissed like it was keeping a secret. I remember thinking: that used to be a tree, and before that it was sunlight, and before that it was the collapse of a star that barfed out carbon, and now it is this strange brown lung baking in winter heat. Nothing had ever been only itself. Everything was a tourist in time, wearing whatever costume the moment required. I didn’t have the vocabulary for “metamorphosis” or “ecology” or “ontological alienation,” but I felt them pressing against my baby teeth like new words trying to hatch.  

That was the first poem I ever wrote: not on paper, but in the chemical theatre of my body. A poem is not made of words; a poem is made of attention. The words are just the coat check ticket you hand to the reader so they can retrieve their own coat on the way out. I have been writing that same poem ever since, even when I’m typing spreadsheets or calculating sales tax. The radiator bag is still in me, shrinking and expanding like a cardio workout for the soul.  

II. The Museum of Unfinished Hooks

We live inside the abandoned chorus of every song we ever started humming and then forgot. Scientists say the auditory cortex keeps singing even after the music stops; the mind is a bar that never kicks anybody out. Walk through any city and you will pass tens of thousands of half-remembered melodies hanging in the air like homeless ghosts, little musical middle children no one adopted. I sometimes picture them crowding around streetlights at dusk, harmonizing in the key of neon, hoping a passing stranger will supply the next line and finally grant them the mercy of conclusion.  

The same thing happens with human encounters. Every conversation that ends mid-sentence—because the subway door sighs shut, because the waiter arrives with the card reader, because someone’s phone blooms into a panic of vibrations—those sentences don’t die. They float above our heads like comic-strip bubbles made of mist. The sum total of unattached beginnings is the psychic smog of modern life. We are marinating in the béchamel of our own orphaned clauses.  

I once tried to collect them. I rode the 6 train with a pocket notebook, and every time I overheard a fragment—“so I told her the hamster was never actually mine”—I wrote it down. After a month I had 217 shards. I spread them on my living-room floor like tarot cards and attempted to build a coherent epic. The result was nonsense, but it was beautiful nonsense, the way a shattered mirror still shows your face in every piece, just displaced and traveling in the wrong direction. The lesson: stories want to remain open. Closure is a colonial enterprise we impose on the wilderness of experience.  

III. The Secret Economy of Dust

Dust is the most democratic substance on Earth. It is skin, pollen, tire shavings, Saharan sand, astronaut dandruff, bonfire ash, the钙化 remains of Jurassic ferns, and the signature of every star that ever exploded. It is the final committee report on everything. Housekeepers wage quiet genocides against it, but dust is pacifist; it simply wishes to be redistributed. When you breathe, you are voting for dust. When you clean, you are staging a coup.  

I keep a small glass vial on my windowsill labeled “Tuesday Dust, 2019.” I do not remember why I collected it, only that I was crying that day and needed evidence that something, anything, had settled. Occasionally I unscrew the cap and peer inside. The particles have arranged themselves into microscopic dunes, miniature Sahara after a microscopic wind. I wonder if they dream of the day they will be inhaled by a future lover, become momentarily part of her arterial symphony, and then exit during a sigh on a balcony overlooking a different city. Dust is the original cryptocurrency: universally exchanged, untraceable, valuable only because everything agrees it exists.  

IV. The Cartography of Forgotten Smells

Our olfactory system plugs directly into the hippocampus, which is why a single whiff of cafeteria pizza can download an entire third-grade Tuesday in 1997, complete with the bruise on your knee and the knowledge that your parents were yelling in the kitchen about money again. Smell is a time machine that runs on fat and nostalgia.  

I am chasing an extinct smell: the interior of my grandmother’s purse in 1985. It was a synthetic leather cube the color of burnt coffee, and its atmosphere contained layers—lint-coated peppermint, the metallic breath of coins, a ghost of Shalimar, the papery panic of supermarket receipts. That smell is gone. The purse itself was probably buried in a New Jersey landfill three decades ago, fermenting into the gestational broth of future fossil fuels. Yet I walk through the world with an internal Geiger counter, hoping for a click. Sometimes I catch a hint in the dollar bin at Target—some off-brand wallet huffs out a weak facsimile and I’m six again, holding her hand as we cross the parking lot, my entire life still a menu of possibilities.  

Smell is the most honest sense because it cannot lie about having been there. Images can be Photoshopped; sounds can be autotuned; touch can misinterpret pressure. But if you smell the salt of someone’s tears on their sweater, that salt has definitely been cried. I want to build a library of lost smells, bottle them like shipwreck messages, and release them in museums so people can collapse in the lobby remembering when they were still children who believed geography was a promise rather than a threat.  

V. The Arithmetic of Insomnia

At 3:12 a.m., time stops being a river and becomes a Rubik’s cube in a dryer: every permutation is equally probable and equally pointless. Insomnia is not the absence of sleep; it is the presence of an extra dimension where everything you ever said is reheard by a jury of your own voice. The ceiling becomes a spreadsheet of unpaid emotional invoices.  

I have devised a game: assign each worry a prime number. 2 for the electricity bill, 3 for the weird mole on my shoulder, 5 for the novel I will never write, 7 for the voicemail from my mother I forgot to return. Multiply them together. The resulting composite number is the exact frequency in hertz at which my spine vibrates. I imagine the number glowing above my head like an esoteric high score in an arcade that only sells despair and chamomile tea. If I could factor the number back into primes, I would supposedly know which worry is the linchpin. But large integers are arrogant; they refuse to break. They stand there, arms crossed, saying, We are your new pantheon, bow.  

Sometimes I get up and stand in front of the open refrigerator, bathing in the photons of a 40-watt bulb that smells like cold plastic. The fridge is the household’s miniature Antarctica, preserving leftovers and grudges at equal temperature. I stare at a jar of pickles and wonder how many night minutes equal one day minute. Is there a currency exchange for temporal refugees? The pickles don’t answer; they just float in their green galaxy, cucumbers that achieved enlightenment by being buried in salt and forgetting the sun.  

VI. The Cartography of Strangers I Almost Loved

There is a woman I see once every few years in the frozen aisle of a specific supermarket. We have never spoken, but our eyes hold a seminar. She always pauses by the peas; I always reach for the same brand of pizza. The fluorescent light makes her look like a deleted scene from Blade Runner. I imagine that she is a forensic astronomer who spends her nights reassembling constellations that were broken by light pollution. She probably imagines I am a divorced dad who teaches philosophy to parrots. Neither of us corrects the other.  

Our non-relationship is perfect because it lacks a plot, and therefore lacks disappointment. It is a love story told in negative space. I once timed my shopping so that I would pass her at the exact moment she reached for the peas. Our carts grazed like shy satellites. My heart performed a drum solo. She looked up; her pupils were dilated like someone had just turned off all the lights in the universe. Then a child screamed, and the moment filed for divorce. I walked away wondering what parallel life we might have shared: arguing about dishwasher etiquette, naming a cactus after a dead poet, taking up salsa dancing at 44.  

I keep a mental atlas of these almosts: the man on the subway whose book I was reading over his shoulder (Bulgakov, always Bulgakov), the barista who drew a sailboat in my latte foam the day I learned my friend had cancer, the woman in the red coat who ran after me to return a glove I dropped but was swallowed by traffic before she could catch up. They are countries I will never visit, whose visas are stamped on the soft tissue behind my ribs.  

VII. The Theology of Parking Lots

Empty parking lots at dawn are the most sacred sites in North America. The yellow stripes are pilgrimage routes for no one; the lampposts are gothic spires without doctrine. Every mall is a Vatican in reverse: cathedrals built to the religion of buying, yet their holiest hour is when they are abandoned, when the asphalt cools and the seagulls pick at yesterday’s churros.  

I used to rollerblade at night in an office-park circuit, coasting past darkened cubicle farms that hummed like beehives on codeine. The motion-sensor lights would click on one by one, a mechanical sunrise triggered by my trespass. I felt like an emperor of fluorescence. I invented a belief system: each light was a soul I had saved by simply noticing it. The more I skated, the larger my congregation. I preached sermons to the shrubs: Blessed are the ergonomic chairs for they shall support your lumbar. Blessed are the vending machines for they provide honey-roast peanuts in the wilderness of deadlines.  

One night I fell. No divine intervention, just a pebble the size of a apostrophe. My knee bled into the concrete, a small red passport stamp. I lay there listening to the building’s air-conditioning breathe for 400 people who were home dreaming of spreadsheets. In that moment I understood that work is just adult hide-and-seek: we disappear into roles all day, then sneak back to ourselves at night. The parking lot is the neutral zone where ghosts clock in and out.  

VIII. The Epistemology of Voicemail

My carrier deletes unheard voicemails after thirty days. This is a cruelty I was never consulted about. Each deletion is a small funeral for a version of me who was absent when the world called. Maybe it was a job offer, maybe a scam about car warranties, maybe my dead friend’s voice accidentally replaying from 2018 because someone butt-dialed my past.  

I save three messages from my mother. In the newest one she says, “Just checking if you ate the leftovers,” her tone floating between intimacy and resignation. I have listened to it 47 times. Scholars of the future, if you find this, know that “leftovers” was a code between us for “I love you but I’ve forgotten the algorithm.” She is alive as I write this, yet the voicemail already feels archival, like a pressed flower anticipating its own museum.  

IX. The Geography of Unfinished Projects

In the hard drive of every creative person there is a folder labeled “misc” that is actually the size of Luxembourg. Inside are half-coded apps, novels that stall at chapter four, synth loops that sound like a migraine trying to apologize, blueprints for birdhouses shaped like famous prisons. We tell ourselves we will return, the way sailors promise their wives they will come back from the sea. The folder grows hair and fingernails in the dark.  

I open mine sometimes like a priest peering into a battlefield hospital. I give last rites to a screenplay about a world where sleep is currency; I dab antiseptic on a spreadsheet of every book I ever meant to read. The files multiply by spores. They are evidence that I have lived many lives in parallel, none to completion. Buddhists say that desire is suffering; hard drives prove that desire is also a clutter problem.  

Yet there is mercy in the misc. One night, drunk on a Tuesday, I dragged two abandoned projects into each other’s folders. A timelapse of clouds merged with a diary I kept during a breakup. The accidental collage looked like heaven getting divorced. It became the only thing I’ve ever made that I can stand to look at for more than ten seconds. Completion is overrated; collision is holy.  

X. The Eschatology of Small Talk

Elevators are the confessionals of the secular world. We step inside and recite catechisms about the weather, the traffic, the latency of the coffee machine. These phrases are not empty; they are protective spells muttered against the intimacy of shared velocity. If the elevator jams, the spell breaks and suddenly we are naked apes weighing the caloric expenditure of cannibalism.  

I once spent 45 minutes trapped in a lift with a man who sold insurance for a living. After we exhausted the liturgy of “sure is hot,” he told me that his daughter could speak to spiders. He said it matter-of-factly, the way people mention they’re allergic to kiwi. The air between us changed texture, became a cathedral. When the doors finally opened, we walked away like survivors of a shipwreck who promised to keep the secret of the island. I never saw him again. Maybe he was a prophet, maybe just a divorced dad high on adrenaline. The story hangs in my elevator like an unpaid toll.  

XI. The Ontology of Left Turns

Every left turn in traffic is a small rebellion against destiny. You are announcing, in front of strangers encased in two-ton exoskeletons, that you refuse to continue forward. The arrow blinks like a Morse code confession: I am going elsewhere, do not follow. Entire lives pivot on such turns. I missed the left that would have taken me to the hospital the night my father died. Instead I went to buy batteries. By the time I arrived, he was a body wearing the face of someone I almost recognized.  

Now, whenever I approach that intersection, the air pressure changes. The ghost of the turn I didn’t take materializes like a hitchhiker. I roll down the window; he stares at me with my own eyes but older, haggard from living the life I didn’t. We never speak. We already know how the conversation ends: with the same silence, just distributed differently.  

XII. The Phenomenology of Rain on Different Surfaces

Rain on tin awnings is applause for a play that never began.  
Rain on car hoods is the world typing a love letter in Morse.  
Rain on cemetery grass is the dead drinking through their skin.  
Rain on a notebook left open on a café table is the sky editing your poem without permission.  
Rain on a stranger’s umbrella is a brief affair that ends when the light changes.  

I keep a mental index of these acoustic micro-cultures. Someday, when the climate collapses and we are rationed three showers a week, we will gather under makeshift awnings just to listen to the memory of water hitting metal. Children will ask, What does abundance sound like? We will cup our ears and replay the applause.  

XIII. The Ethics of Shadows

At 5:17 p.m. in late October, my shadow is seven feet tall and wants to leave me. It stretches toward the horizon like a passport applicant. I can feel it tugging at the soles of my shoes, petitioning for emancipation. Who could blame it? I have dragged it across failed relationships, unpaid invoices, and the gum-blotched sidewalks of too many cities.  

Shadows are loyal but not faithful; they will mimic you only as long as the light allows. Turn the light off and they immediately file for divorce. Buddhists might call this non-attachment; I call it betrayal. Yet on overcast days we walk together like old friends who have run out of things to say. The cloud is a marriage counselor telling us to compromise: you can stay connected, but neither of you gets to be real.  

XIV. The Metaphysics of Thrift Stores

Second-hand shops are purgatories where objects await reincarnation. A coffee mug that once said “World’s Okayest Dad” now houses a cactus in a dorm room; a wedding dress hangs next to a bowling trophy, exchanging origin stories in whispers. I buy shirts there not for the fabric but for the residual dreams. One T-shirt had a faded print of a 5K race from 2003. When I wore it to bed, I dreamed I was running through a tunnel of strangers cheering for someone I had never been. I woke up with shin splints and a vague sense of guilt.  

Volunteers who sort the donations are the unpaid ferrymen of Hades. They decide what crosses the river into a new life and what gets balled into textile coffins. I asked one if she ever felt like a god. She said, “Only when I find half a twenty in a coat pocket. Then I buy lottery tickets for the crew.” Salvation is recursive.  

XV. The Afterlife of Unsent Letters

I write letters to people who are still alive but with whom I have no forwarding address. The letters live in a shoebox under the bed, sealed like evidence of crimes I chose not to commit. One begins: “I loved the way you mispronounced ‘haphazard,’ as if the word itself were falling down stairs.” Another ends: “If you ever need a kidney, ask someone else; I already gave you my twenties.”  

Psychologists say unsent letters are therapeutic; I say they are literary mummies. They preserve the emotional corpse so it doesn’t stink up the living room. Sometimes, late at night, I fan them out like tarot cards and ask the universe for a sign. The universe responds with silence, which is its own sort of signature: I have read receipt turned off.  

XVI. The Cosmology of Checkout Lines

The queue at the grocery store is the last place where randomness is tolerated. You can choose your lane, but not the person ahead of you who will pay in dimes, or the baby who will vomit avocado down your calf. Algorithms haven’t yet figured out how to monetize the existential pause between “paper or plastic” and “have a nice day.” Cherish this lag; it might be the final free zone.  

I once stood behind an elderly man buying a single banana and a bottle of bleach. Nothing else. The combination was so ominous that the teenage clerk stalled, weighing the moral geometry of the sale. The man turned to me and winked: “Don’t worry, son. The banana is for me. The bleach is for the world.” He paid in quarters and left. I think about him whenever the news anchor says “unprecedented.”  

XVII. The Symmetry of Last Pages

A 2,500-word limit is a literary parking meter ticking toward zero. You feed it quarters of meaning, watching the red flag creep upward. The temptation is to floor the accelerator, to tie every loose thread into a bouquet that proves I was here. But the truth is that essays, like lives, end mid-sentence. The only honest conclusion is to stop.  

So I leave you here, halfway up the down escalator, holding a paper bag that once was a tree, breathing dust that once was a star, listening to rain that might be applause or might be punctuation. Your shadow is stretching toward another city; your unsent letters are breeding in the dark. The elevator is jammed between floors, and someone inside is telling a stranger that spiders know his daughter’s name.  

Go left.  
Or don’t.  
The radiator is still hissing.

Now, whenever I approach that intersection, the air pressure changes. The ghost of the turn I didn’t take materializes like a hitchhiker. I roll down the window; he stares at me with my own eyes but older, haggard from living the life I didn’t. We never speak. We already know how the conversation ends: with the same silence, just distributed differently.  

XII. The Phenomenology of Rain on Different Surfaces

Rain on tin awnings is applause for a play that never began.  
Rain on car hoods is the world typing a love letter in Morse.  
Rain on cemetery grass is the dead drinking through their skin.  
Rain on a notebook left open on a café table is the sky editing your poem without permission.  
Rain on a stranger’s umbrella is a brief affair that ends when the light changes.  

I keep a mental index of these acoustic micro-cultures. Someday, when the climate collapses and we are rationed three showers a week, we will gather under makeshift awnings just to listen to the memory of water hitting metal. Children will ask, What does abundance sound like? We will cup our ears and replay the applause.  

XIII. The Ethics of Shadows

At 5:17 p.m. in late October, my shadow is seven feet tall and wants to leave me. It stretches toward the horizon like a passport applicant. I can feel it tugging at the soles of my shoes, petitioning for emancipation. Who could blame it? I have dragged it across failed relationships, unpaid invoices, and the gum-blotched sidewalks of too many cities.  

Shadows are loyal but not faithful; they will mimic you only as long as the light allows. Turn the light off and they immediately file for divorce. Buddhists might call this non-attachment; I call it betrayal. Yet on overcast days we walk together like old friends who have run out of things to say. The cloud is a marriage counselor telling us to compromise: you can stay connected, but neither of you gets to be real.  

XIV. The Metaphysics of Thrift Stores

Second-hand shops are purgatories where objects await reincarnation. A coffee mug that once said “World’s Okayest Dad” now houses a cactus in a dorm room; a wedding dress hangs next to a bowling trophy, exchanging origin stories in whispers. I buy shirts there not for the fabric but for the residual dreams. One T-shirt had a faded print of a 5K race from 2003. When I wore it to bed, I dreamed I was running through a tunnel of strangers cheering for someone I had never been. I woke up with shin splints and a vague sense of guilt.  

Volunteers who sort the donations are the unpaid ferrymen of Hades. They decide what crosses the river into a new life and what gets balled into textile coffins. I asked one if she ever felt like a god. She said, “Only when I find half a twenty in a coat pocket. Then I buy lottery tickets for the crew.” Salvation is recursive.  

XV. The Afterlife of Unsent Letters

I write letters to people who are still alive but with whom I have no forwarding address. The letters live in a shoebox under the bed, sealed like evidence of crimes I chose not to commit. One begins: “I loved the way you mispronounced ‘haphazard,’ as if the word itself were falling down stairs.” Another ends: “If you ever need a kidney, ask someone else; I already gave you my twenties.”  

Psychologists say unsent letters are therapeutic; I say they are literary mummies. They preserve the emotional corpse so it doesn’t stink up the living room. Sometimes, late at night, I fan them out like tarot cards and ask the universe for a sign. The universe responds with silence, which is its own sort of signature: I have read receipt turned off.  

XVI. The Cosmology of Checkout Lines

The queue at the grocery store is the last place where randomness is tolerated. You can choose your lane, but not the person ahead of you who will pay in dimes, or the baby who will vomit avocado down your calf. Algorithms haven’t yet figured out how to monetize the existential pause between “paper or plastic” and “have a nice day.” Cherish this lag; it might be the final free zone.  

I once stood behind an elderly man buying a single banana and a bottle of bleach. Nothing else. The combination was so ominous that the teenage clerk stalled, weighing the moral geometry of the sale. The man turned to me and winked: “Don’t worry, son. The banana is for me. The bleach is for the world.” He paid in quarters and left. I think about him whenever the news anchor says “unprecedented.”  

XVII. The Symmetry of Last Pages

A 2,500-word limit is a literary parking meter ticking toward zero. You feed it quarters of meaning, watching the red flag creep upward. The temptation is to floor the accelerator, to tie every loose thread into a bouquet that proves I was here. But the truth is that essays, like lives, end mid-sentence. The only honest conclusion is to stop.  

So I leave you here, halfway up the down escalator, holding a paper bag that once was a tree, breathing dust that once was a star, listening to rain that might be applause or might be punctuation. Your shadow is stretching toward another city; your unsent letters are breeding in the dark. The elevator is jammed between floors, and someone inside is telling a stranger that spiders know his daughter’s name.  

Go left.  
Or don’t.  
The radiator is still hissing.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
